content:
# Luke Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 88-89

---

**1** Jesus returned from the Jordan, overflowing with the Holy Spirit, and the Spirit led him into the wilderness, **2** where for forty days the devil put him to the test. He ate nothing during those days, and when they were over he was starving.

**3** The devil said to him, "If you are God's Son, tell this stone to become bread."

**4** Jesus answered, "It is written: 'A person cannot live on bread alone.'"

**5** Then the devil took him up to a high place and showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a single moment. **6** The devil said to him, "I will hand over to you all this power and splendor — it has been given to me, and I can give it to anyone I choose. **7** So if you will bow down before me, it will all be yours."

**8** Jesus answered him, "It is written: 'Worship the Lord your God and serve him alone.'"

**9** Then the devil brought him to Jerusalem, set him on the highest point of the temple, and said, "If you are God's Son, throw yourself down from here. **10** For it is written: 'He will command his angels to protect you,' **11** and 'They will carry you in their hands, so you will not even strike your foot against a stone.'"

**12** Jesus answered, "It says: 'Do not put the Lord your God to the test.'"

**13** When the devil had exhausted every kind of temptation, he withdrew from him until a more opportune time.

**14** Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit, and news about him spread through the entire region. **15** He taught in their synagogues, and everyone spoke highly of him.

**16** He came to Nazareth, where he had been raised. On the Sabbath day he went to the synagogue, as was his custom, and stood up to read. **17** The scroll of the prophet Isaiah was handed to him. He unrolled it and found the passage where it was written:

**18** *"The Spirit of the Lord is upon me,*
*because he has anointed me*
*to bring good news to the poor.*
*He has sent me to announce freedom for captives*
*and recovery of sight for the blind,*
*to set free those who are crushed,*
**19** *to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor."*

**20** Then he rolled up the scroll, gave it back to the attendant, and sat down. Every eye in the synagogue was fixed on him. **21** He began by saying to them, "Today, right here as you listen, this scripture has come true."

**22** Everyone spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his mouth. They said, "Isn't this Joseph's son?"

**23** He said to them, "No doubt you will quote this proverb at me: 'Doctor, heal yourself!' and say, 'Do here in your hometown what we have heard you did in Capernaum.'"

**24** Then he said, "I tell you the truth — no prophet is welcomed in his own hometown.

**25** "Believe me, there were many widows in Israel in Elijah's day, when the sky was shut for three and a half years and severe famine struck the whole land. **26** Yet Elijah was not sent to any of them, but to a widow in Zarephath, in the region of Sidon. **27** And there were many people with leprosy in Israel in the time of the prophet Elisha, yet not one of them was healed — only Naaman the Syrian."

**28** When they heard this, everyone in the synagogue was filled with fury. **29** They jumped to their feet, drove him out of the town, and took him to the brow of the hill on which their town was built, intending to hurl him off the cliff. **30** But he walked straight through the middle of the crowd and went on his way.

**31** He went down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and on the Sabbath he began teaching the people. **32** They were astounded at his teaching, because his words carried authority.

**33** In the synagogue there was a man possessed by the spirit of an unclean demon, and he screamed at the top of his voice, **34** "Ha! What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!"

**35** Jesus rebuked him sharply: "Be silent and come out of him!" The demon threw the man down in front of them all and came out without harming him.

**36** Everyone was stunned. They said to one another, "What kind of word is this? He commands unclean spirits with authority and power, and they come out!" **37** And the news about him spread to every corner of the surrounding region.

**38** Jesus left the synagogue and went to Simon's house. Now Simon's mother-in-law was suffering from a severe fever, and they asked Jesus to help her. **39** He stood over her, rebuked the fever, and it left her. She got up at once and began serving them.

**40** As the sun was setting, everyone who had people sick with various diseases brought them to Jesus. He laid his hands on each one and healed them. **41** Demons also came out of many people, shrieking, "You are the Son of God!" But he rebuked them and would not let them speak, because they knew he was the Christ.

**42** At daybreak, Jesus went out to a deserted place. The crowds went searching for him, and when they found him they tried to keep him from leaving. **43** But he told them, "I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well — that is why I was sent."

**44** And he kept on preaching in the synagogues of Judea.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **4:1 — "overflowing with the Holy Spirit"**: The Greek *pleres pneumatos hagiou* (full of the Holy Spirit) is rendered dynamically to convey the active, empowering sense — not merely a static condition but an animating fullness that drives Jesus into the wilderness.

2. **4:4 — Omission of "but by every word of God"**: Codex Sinaiticus omits the second half of the Deuteronomy 8:3 quotation ("but by every word of God") that appears in many later manuscripts. The PNEUMA translation follows the Sinaiticus reading faithfully.

3. **4:5-8 — Temptation order**: Sinaiticus follows the Lukan order (kingdoms, then temple), which differs from Matthew's order. This is preserved as found in the manuscript.

4. **4:13 — "a more opportune time"**: Greek *achri kairou* — literally "until a season." The dynamic rendering captures the sinister strategic quality: the devil retreats tactically, not permanently.

5. **4:18-19 — Isaiah quotation**: This is Jesus' programmatic declaration of mission (Isaiah 61:1-2, with 58:6 woven in). The poetic formatting reflects that this is Scripture being read aloud, a prophetic proclamation. Sinaiticus includes the phrase about recovery of sight for the blind.

6. **4:20 — "Every eye in the synagogue was fixed on him"**: Greek *atenizo* means to gaze intently, with fixed attention. The dynamic rendering captures the dramatic tension of the moment — the entire congregation staring, waiting.

7. **4:21 — "right here as you listen"**: Greek *en tois osin humon* is literally "in your ears." The dynamic translation conveys the immediacy: fulfillment is happening in the very act of hearing.

8. **4:22 — "gracious words"**: Greek *logoi tes charitos* — "words of grace." This captures both the content (grace) and the manner (eloquence, beauty) of Jesus' speech.

9. **4:29 — "hurl him off the cliff"**: Greek *katakremnizo* — to throw down a precipice. The dynamic translation makes the murderous intent viscerally clear.

10. **4:32 — "his words carried authority"**: Greek *en exousia en ho logos autou* — literally "in authority was his word." The dynamic rendering conveys that the authority was not external but intrinsic to his speech itself.

11. **4:34 — "Ha!"**: Greek *ea* — an exclamation of alarm or recognition. Rendered as "Ha!" to preserve the startled, involuntary quality of the demonic cry.

12. **4:44 — "synagogues of Judea"**: Sinaiticus reads *Ioudaias* (Judea) rather than *Galilaias* (Galilee) found in some later manuscripts. This is a significant Sinaiticus variant. The PNEUMA translation follows the manuscript as read, understanding "Judea" in its broader sense as the land of the Jewish people, encompassing Galilee.
